Case title: AgentSociety and generative social simulation as a transportability boundary case
Short description: Use large-scale LLM social simulation to test whether calibration and transportability become separate human roles when simulated societies are used for policy insight.
Empirical unit of analysis: Simulated agent interactions, policy interventions, and comparisons to real-world patterns
Observable human actors: Model builder, social scientist, policy analyst
Candidate roles tested: Calibration architect; counterfactual and transportability judge
Key typology question: Does simulation quality force a split between calibration work and broader transportability judgment?
Observable artifacts / traces: Simulation logs, parameter settings, validation comparisons, intervention outputs
Key outcomes observed: Alignment with real-world patterns, failure under distribution shift, policy relevance
What would weaken / falsify the role cla: Simulation outputs transport reliably enough that calibration and transportability collapse into one role
Main confounds / risks of misreading: Agent-based simulations may look realistic while still missing institutional mechanisms
Why this case has high leverage: It is a strong pressure test for predictive-role splitting beyond surveys and experiments.
collector_notes: Generated from workbook and workbook-linked sources.
